originalTitle: Democrats criticize Trump’s new tariffs as ‘end-run around the supreme court and Congress’ – US politics live
summary: Canada, Mexico, Australia, EU, UK and China among dozens of economies listed in US president’s renewed ⁠effort of near-global import taxes after supreme court struck down his ‘liberation day’ tariffs
content: Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, told reporters that “everything’s on the table” in response to new tariffs imposed on his country this week by Donald Trump. Canada will do “whatever it takes to defend and support our families, our workers and our businesses,” he added. The premier of Ontario, Doug Ford, was less diplomatic when asked if Canada has been “bringing a knife to a gun fight” so far” in responding to Trump’s trade war. “I wouldn’t say that,” Ford replied, “but it’s very hard to deal with President Trump when he changes his mind every single day. One day the prime minister is with him side by side, and next thing you know, President Trump turns on him like a rabid dog.” Matthew Holmes, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce’s chief of public policy, said in a statement that it was suspicious that the US suddenly imposed tariffs on Canada for supposedly not doing enough on forced labor one day before a previous round of short-term tariffs is due to expire. “Canada is a leader on this with a formal prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labour, more legislation coming to strengthen this regime, and businesses investing significantly in compliance, due diligence and supply chain transparency,” Holmes said. “Canada should not be targeted here.” “If the intent is truly to address forced labour, the focus should be a coordinated approach through a multilateral mechanism. The timing of this is somewhat suspect as previous rounds of tariffs sunset,” he added. “Trump is hell-bent on raising your prices,” congressman Brendan Boyle, a Pennsylvania Democrat said in a statement on Donald Trump’s lastest tariffs on 85 countries. “His new tariffs are a nationwide sales tax that will only serve to make life harder for Americans just trying to make ends meet.” The Pentagon’s official online list of service members killed in the ongoing Iran war does not include the four soldiers who died during renewed fighting over the weekend, despite the fact that their names were included in a Pentagon press release on the formal transfer of their remains back to the United States on Wednesday. A review of the current list on the Pentagon’s Defense Casualty Analysis System website shows that, despite having been last updated on Wednesday, the number of US military personnel killed during Operation Epic Fury, the Pentagon’s name for the US-Israeli war on Iran Donald Trump launched on 28 February, remains at 14. A separate “Names of the Fallen” list on the same Pentagon casualties site currently includes just 13 service members. According to a New York Times report, the Pentagon website listed 18 American service members had been killed during the war on Iran on Wednesday, but was changed on Thursday to exclude three service members killed last Friday, Saturday and Sunday as Iran retaliated for new US strikes with an attack on a base used by US forces in Jordan. The list also excluded was a fourth soldier who was killed during a controlled detonation of an Iranian drone at Erbil air base in Iraq. By Thursday, the Defense Department had lowered that number, reporting that 14 American troops had been killed in the war. Three military officials told the Times “that one reason behind the change was that the Trump administration decided to remove four service members killed this past weekend from the list … because their deaths occurred after President Trump declared a cease-fire in the war in April.” A Pentagon spokesman disputed that contention, telling the Times that the decreased total of war dead was die to “temporary data disruptions” that would be quickly fixed. The reported removal of the recent war dead from the Pentagon list comes one day after Trump traveled to Dover air force base in Delaware to salute the remains of the four soldiers killed in the past week and as he continues to demand credit for the number of dead being so much lower than in years-long conflicts like the US wars in Korea, Vietnam, Iraq and Afghanistan. On Wednesday, Trump posted a chart on his social media platform comparing the death toll of 18 in his Iran war with those previous wars, which misstated the number killed in Iraq and Afghanistan, with the caption: “These are the real facts. REPORT THEM!” Speaking to reporters on Thursday, on his way to what the Pentagon calls the “dignified transfer” of the killed service members’ remains, Trump claimed that the dead soldiers had supported his claim that the war on Iran is necessary to prevent that nation from developing a nuclear weapons – an effort US intelligence assessed Iran abandoned in 2003. “We’re going to Dover,” Trump said, “to honor our heroes and they’re indeed great heroes. All of them said, very strongly, that we can’t let Iran have a nuclear weapon”. At an event on Thursday, Trump again argued that the death toll from his war on Iran was low compared to that of Vietnam, the war he did not serve in as a young man. “You know, we were in Vietnam for 20 years, lost thousands and hundreds of thousands of lives,” Trump said, inflating the actual death toll of 58,220. “We were in Afghanistan for years. We were in all these wars that you heard about for years and years,” Trump added. “Well, we’re in for four months and we- I had a very sad day yesterday; I went to Dover, four great American patriots killed. That’s 18 … and one is too many, but it’s 18. Whereas in Vietnam, we lost 200,000.” Donald Trump has suggested in a social-media post that damage caused to shipping by Iranian attacks could be paid for using Tehran’s assets frozen overseas. Please let this statement serve to represent, until further notice, that from this point forth, any and all damages done to Ships, Cargo, or anything related thereto, will be paid for by Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls. It’s unclear whether the US would be able to use frozen assets in such a way; any move would probably be challenged. Europe has faced an array of issues in its
chineseTitle: 民主党人批评特朗普的新关税是“绕过最高法院和国会”——美国政治直播
chineseSummary: 在最高法院推翻“解放日”关税后，美国总统重新启动近乎全球的进口税，其中包括加拿大、墨西哥、澳大利亚、欧盟、英国和中国等数十个经济体
chineseContent: 加拿大总理马克·卡尼告诉记者，针对唐纳德·特朗普本周对加拿大征收的新关税，“一切都已摆在桌面上”。他补充说，加拿大将“尽一切努力捍卫和支持我们的家庭、工人和企业”。当被问及加拿大迄今为止是否“以刀赴枪”来应对特朗普的贸易战时，安大略省省长道格·福特(Doug Ford)的态度就没那么外交了。“我不会这么说，”福特回答说，“但当特朗普总统每天都改变主意时，与他打交道是非常困难的。”有一天，首相与他并肩而行，接下来你知道的，特朗普总统像一只疯狗一样向他发起攻击。”加拿大商会公共政策负责人马修·霍姆斯在一份声明中表示，美国在上一轮短期关税到期前一天突然对加拿大征收关税，据称是因为加拿大在强迫劳动问题上做得不够。霍姆斯说：“加拿大在这方面处于领先地位，正式禁止进口强迫劳动生产的商品，更多立法将加强这一制度，企业在合规性、尽职调查和供应链透明度方面进行了大量投资。” “加拿大不应该成为这里的目标。” “如果真正的目的是解决强迫劳动问题，那么重点应该是通过多边机制采取协调一致的方法。这一时机有些可疑，因为前几轮关税已经结束。”他补充道。宾夕法尼亚州民主党众议员布伦丹·博伊尔(Brendan Boyle)在一份关于唐纳德·特朗普对85个国家最新关税的声明中表示，“特朗普一意孤行地要提高你们的价格。他的新关税是一项全国性的销售税，只会让那些只是想维持生计的美国人的生活变得更加困难。”
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
